narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, an LSAT score of 21.50, and strong early academic performance where you ranked in the 8th decile of your school in Year 1, though that slipped to the 4th decile by Year 3, alongside coming from a middle-income family and planning full-time studies. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 45% chance you'll fail the bar—high enough that it tipped the decision against your application, as schools prioritize applicants likely to pass and succeed professionally. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score, which stands well below the typical 36.70 average and signaled to the model a notable risk compared to students who passed. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA, though slightly under the 3.21 average, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction toward success, showing some academic resilience. Even stronger was your Year 1 decile ranking, which outperformed the average of 5.71 and pulled hardest against a failure outcome, highlighting your early potential. These top three elements—your LSAT as the biggest concern, tempered by solid early grades and a decent GPA—ultimately outweighed other details like family income or study plans in shaping the model's close-call decision. While your background as a Hispanic male brings valuable diversity that schools value, the model focused strictly on bar passage signals here. Compared to typical admitted students, your profile shows promise in early performance but gaps in standardized testing that mirrored struggles for others who failed the bar. This isn't a judgment on your worth or determination—it's one data-driven view from a tool designed to minimize risks for everyone involved. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to average, perhaps with targeted prep that leverages your early-grade strengths, and seek feedback from advisors on bolstering your Year 3 performance narrative. Reapplying with those improvements, or exploring programs with holistic reviews, could shift the odds significantly in your favor next time. You're clearly capable, and with focused steps, this setback can become a stepping stone.
